title: ‘Use it or lose it’: Super tax break that is closing soon
full_text:
Superannuation has rarely looked more attractive as a wealth-building vehicle than it does right now. While the federal budget left super largely untouched, it introduced higher taxes on trusts and most other investment assets, sending many investors searching for shelter.
For those with the financial capacity to act, super is fast emerging as the structure of choice.
But most have only days left to maximise their position before this financial year closes.
Super savers will soon lose the ability to make tax-deductible catch-up contributions for the 2020-21 financial year – a period when millions of Australians earned less money and had less funnelling into their super.
Catch-up contributions, officially called carry forward contributions, allow savers to make extra tax-deductible concessional contributions of unused cap space from up to five previous financial years. The catch is that super savers must first use the current financial year’s concessional contributions cap of $30,000, but for people with the financial firepower to do so, catch-up contributions deliver big tax benefits.
Caveo Partners chief economist and financial strategist Theo Marinis says super is “one of the very few things that wasn’t impacted by the budget, so pouring more money into super makes sense”.
He said catch-up contributions made sense for people whose taxable income was more than $45,000, putting them in the 32 per cent tax bracket.
“Why pay 32 per cent tax when I can pay 15 per cent by putting it into super?” Marinis says. “You save 17c in the dollar net. If you put $10,000 you are going to save $1700 even after the contributions tax.” People on higher marginal tax rates get even bigger benefits by making tax-deductible super contributions. “If you don’t use it, once the five years are over, you lose it,” Marinis says. “Most super funds have a close-off date because they are inundated with people putting money in. Sometimes it’s as early as the middle of June, but it tends to be the week before the end of June. If you get your contribution in after their close-off date, they will count it in July and you can’t claim it. Check with your individual fund when their close-off date is to receive contributions this year.” Super Consumers Australia chief executive Xavier O’Halloran says compulsory super does a lot of the heavy lifting for younger people, but for older Australians who have not enjoyed decades of high employer contributions, currently 12 per cent of wages, “extra contributions can help”.
“If you’re looking to make personal contributions this financial year, check with your super fund or the ATO to see what space you have left in your contributions cap,” O’Halloran says.
“The cap rolls over a five-year period, so for people who were out of paid employment around the time of Covid, this is an opportunity to make up for any contributions you missed.” Vanguard Australia chief of personal investor Renae Smith says many people assume that if they miss contributing in one year, the opportunity is lost. “But the carry-forward rule means those missed contributions can potentially be used later, when people are better placed financially,” Smith says.
Vanguard has calculated that over the last five years, someone who has not used their concessional contribution caps could have accumulated up to $137,500 in unused contribution capacity, with the 2025-26 cap of $30,000 taking the total to $167,500 of potentially tax-deductible contributions this year. It says the carry-forward rule is generally available to individuals with a total super balance below $500,000.
“Members interested in making a lump sum contribution from their take-home pay should do it at least a week before June 30 to allow time for processing,” Smith says. “To claim a tax deduction, you’ll also need to complete a ‘notice of intent’ form and submit it to your super fund before lodging your tax return.” Vanguard research has found that 46 per cent of Australians have never made an additional contribution to their super. Hesta chief executive Debby Blakey says cost-of-living pressures mean top-up contributions might not be suitable for everyone, but there were other options to consider.
“Small steps can help, like seeing if you’re eligible for the government co-contribution, consolidating old accounts or considering your retirement strategy,” Blakey says. Hesta research has found only one quarter were aware of catch-up contributions, spouse contributions or contribution splitting.
